1: Eliphaz's third speech opens with the standard formula. This is his most direct speech: he abandons general argument and launches specific accusations against Job.
2: Hăyiskōn lĕʾēl gāber: 'Can a human be of use to God?' — sākan (to be useful/profitable, to have advantage): the commercial term applied to the human-divine relationship. Eliphaz's argument: since humans cannot profit God, God's judgment of Job cannot be motivated by self-interest — it must be pure justice responding to Job's sin. The logic will be refuted in 40:1-5.
3: Ḥēpeṣ lĕšadday: 'Does the Almighty take pleasure?' — ḥāpeṣ (desire, delight, pleasure) is the will-term. Biṣdāqĕtĕkā (in your being righteous) and tĕtammîm dĕrākêkā (in your walking blameless paths): these are the Job-identity terms from 1:1. Eliphaz implicitly denies that God would care about such righteousness — a position God directly refutes in the prologue and epilogue.
4: Hayirʾātĕkā: 'Is it because of your fear/reverence?' — the sarcastic pivot: Eliphaz claims Job's trouble cannot be divine response to his piety. Yāḇôʾ ʿimmĕkā bammišpāṭ: 'he enters into judgment with you' — the legal term mišpāṭ (judgment, legal proceeding). Eliphaz's claim: God would not bother with a lawsuit just because Job is pious.
5: Rabbāh rāʿātĕkā: 'Your wickedness is immense' — the direct accusation. Rabbāh (great, immense) and ʾên-qēṣ (without end): the unprecedented scope of the charges Eliphaz is about to fabricate. The hyperbole reveals the poverty of his evidence.
6: Taḥbol ʾaḥêkā ḥinnām: 'You seized collateral from your kinsmen without reason' — ḥābal (to take a pledge/collateral): the legal-economic act of debt-collateral, regulated in Deut 24:10-13 to protect the poor. Ḥinnām (for nothing, without cause): the same word used of the adversary's challenge in 1:9 — Job suffered ḥinnām; now Eliphaz accuses Job of sinning ḥinnām. Tĕpassēṭ lĕḇûšîm: 'you stripped people of clothes' — the specific violation of Deut 24:12-13.
7: Mayim ʿāyēp lōʾ hišqîtāh: 'You have not given water to the weary' — the specific breach of desert hospitality, a capital moral failure in the ANE. Lāʾeh (weary traveler) and rāʿēb (starving/hungry): the two most vulnerable conditions. These charges have no basis in the narrative — Eliphaz invents them to fit his retributive scheme.
8: ʾîš zĕrôaʿ lô hāʾāreṣ: 'The man of strong arm possessed the land' — zĕrôaʿ (arm/strength) as the means of land acquisition by force. Nĕśûʾ pānîm (man of high standing/lifted face): social status that enables land-seizure. Eliphaz implies Job was such a man — benefiting from structural injustice.
9: ʾAlmānôt šillaḥtā rêqām: 'You have sent away widows empty-handed' — ʾalmānāh (widow) is one of the covenant-care triad (orphan, widow, stranger) whose protection defines covenant faithfulness (Deut 10:18; 14:29). Zĕrōʿôt yĕtōmîm yĕdukkāʾ: 'the strength of orphans is crushed' — dāḵāʾ (to crush) — the most vulnerable social categories systematically oppressed. These are the very categories Job claims to have protected (29:12-16).
10: Pāḥîm sĕḇîḇôtêkā: 'That is why traps are all around you' — the causal connector: Eliphaz's retributive logic applied. Paḥaḏ piṯʾōm yĕḇahălekā: 'sudden terror terrifies you' — paḥad piṯʾōm (sudden terror/dread): the paired wisdom term for divine surprise-judgment (Prov 3:25; Isa 47:11).
11: ʾôr lōʾ-tirʾeh: 'darkness so dense you cannot see' — light is the metaphor for divine presence and moral clarity. Šeṭep mayim yĕkassekā: 'a flood of waters sweeps over you' — the flood-judgment image (cf. Ps 69:1-2). Eliphaz applies the full weight of covenant-curse vocabulary to Job's condition.
12: ʾĔlôah gōbah šāmayim: 'Is not God in the heights of heaven?' — the rhetorical question sets up Eliphaz's critique of Job's implied theology. Rōʾš kôkābîm: 'the top of the stars' — the highest point of the created order. Eliphaz is preparing to accuse Job of thinking God is too remote to see human affairs.
13: Wĕʾāmartā mah-yāḏaʿ ʾēl: 'And you say, What does God know?' — Eliphaz puts these words in Job's mouth; Job never said them. ʾărāpel yišpōṭ: 'Can he judge through the dense cloud?' — ʿărāpel (deep darkness, storm-cloud): the same word used for the theophanic cloud at Sinai (Exod 20:21). Eliphaz inverts the theophanic vocabulary into an accusation of Job's supposed theology.
14: ʿābîm sēter-lô: 'clouds screen him' — Eliphaz attributes to Job the belief that clouds prevent divine vision. Ḥûg šāmayim yithallāk: 'he walks only along the circuit of the sky' — ḥûg (circle, vault): the word used in 26:10 and Isa 40:22 for the circle of creation. Eliphaz reduces the transcendent God to a distant walker on the sky-vault.
15: Hăhăšmōr ʾōraḥ ʿôlām: 'Will you keep to the old path?' — ʾōraḥ ʿôlām (ancient path/road of ages): the road of the wicked from of old. The implied accusation: Job is repeating the path of the pre-flood wicked.
16: Lōʾ-ʿēt quṣṣāpû: 'cut down before their time' — qāṣap (to cut off prematurely): the violent early death of the wicked as confirmation of guilt. Yûṣaq nāhār yĕsōḏām: 'a river poured out their foundation' — flood-judgment as historical paradigm (the flood generation, Gen 6-7).
17: ʾĂšer ʾāmĕrû lāʾēl: 'those who said to God, Leave us alone' — the refusal of divine claim, echoing the wicked generation's rejection. Mah-yišʿad šadday lāhem: 'What can the Almighty do to us?' — divine impotence asserted as basis for autonomous living. Eliphaz links Job's protests to this ancient rebellion.
18: Wĕhûʾ millēʾ ḇāttêhem ṭôb: 'Yet he had filled their houses with abundance' — the paradox of the wicked prospered by God. ʿAṣat rĕšāʿîm rāḥăqāh mimmennî: 'the counsel of the wicked is far from me' — Eliphaz distances himself from the implications of his own observation; the statement begins to undermine his retributive case.
19: Yirʾû ṣaddîqîm wĕyiśmāḥû: 'the righteous see their destruction and celebrate' — the divine reversal: the community of the righteous witnesses the fall of the wicked. Nāqî yilaʿag lāmô: 'the innocent man mocks them' — lāʿag (to mock, scoff): the derisive laughter of vindication.
20: ʾIm-lōʾ nikkĕdū qîmānû: 'Surely our opponents are destroyed' — the confession of the righteous community. ʾEšām ʾāḵlāh ʾēš: 'what remained of them the fire has devoured' — the totality of destruction. Eliphaz is sketching a picture of where Job's path leads.
21: Haskēn-nāʾ ʿimmô: 'Make peace with him now' — sākan returns: the same word from v2 (to be of use/profit) now means 'to be at peace/reconciled with.' The commercial term captures Eliphaz's transactional theology: reconciliation is profitable. Wĕḇāhem tāḇôʾ tôḇātĕkā: 'in that way good will come to you' — moralistic promise of conditional prosperity.
22: Qĕḥal-nāʾ mippiyw tôrāh: 'Accept his instruction from his own mouth' — qāḥal (to receive, take) + tôrāh (instruction, teaching, law): the wisdom-reception formula. Śîm ʾămārāyw bĕlibbĕkā: 'store his words in your heart' — the Torah-internalization command (Deut 6:6; Ps 119:11).
23: ʾIm-tāšûḇ ʿad-šadday tibbāneh: 'If you return to the Almighty, you will be restored' — šûb (to return, repent) + bānāh in Niphal (to be built up, restored): the restoration promise is conditional on repentance. Tarḥîq ʿawlāh: 'banish iniquity' — the practical repentance Eliphaz demands.
24: Šît-ʿal-ʿāpār beṣer: 'treat your gold like ordinary dust' — bāṣer (gold/ore/precious metal) treated as ʿāpār (dust/earth/ground): the value-inversion that characterizes genuine repentance. ʾôpîr (Ophir): the famous gold-producing region of uncertain location (1 Kgs 9:28; 10:11), treated like stream-bed pebbles. The point is radical revaluation: when God becomes your wealth, earthly wealth loses its grip.
25: Wĕhāyāh šadday bĕṣārekā: 'The Almighty himself will be your gold' — the great inversion: šadday (Almighty) as bĕṣer (gold/precious metal). Wĕkesep tôʿăpôt lāk: 'abundance of silver to you' — Eliphaz's invitation, however misapplied to Job, contains genuine theological insight: God himself as the supreme treasure replaces material wealth.
26: Kî-ʾāz ʿal-šadday titʿannāg: 'Then you will take delight in the Almighty' — ʿānag (to delight, take pleasure in) with God as the object: cf. Ps 37:4 ('delight yourself in the Lord'). Tiśśāʾ ʾel-ʾĕlôah pānêkā: 'lift your face toward God' — the gesture of confidence before God, the opposite of shame (cf. Ezra 9:6).
27: Teʿtar ʾēlāyw weyišmāʿekā: 'You will pray and he will hear you' — ʿātar (to pray fervently, implore) — the same root as the intercessory prayer of Job for his friends in 42:8-10. Wĕnĕḏārêkā tĕšallēm: 'you will fulfill your vows' — the completion of worship promised upon restoration. Eliphaz's promise accidentally describes the final chapter's reality (42:7-10).
28: Wĕtiqmōṣ mimmennî mišpāṭ ʿanî: 'Whatever you determine will be settled' — qāmaṣ is difficult here; the MKT renders it as the righteous person's decisions taking effect. ʿal-dĕrākêkā nōgah ʾôr: 'light will shine on your paths' — the path-imagery of Proverbs wisdom, here promised upon repentance.
29: Kî-hišpîlû wattōʾmer gēwāh: 'When others are brought low, you will say, There is hope' — gēwāh (pride/height, here: lifting-up, elevation — i.e., hope): when others experience humiliation, the restored person speaks words of hope. Wĕyôšaʿ šĕpaḥ ʿênāyim: 'he will save the one who bows his eyes' — the reversal pattern: the lowly exalted.
30: Yĕmallēṭ ʾî-nāqî: 'He will rescue even the one who is not innocent' — a remarkable statement for Eliphaz: even the not-innocent can be rescued through the purity of another. Wĕnimlat bĕbōr kappêkā: 'he will be delivered through the cleanness/purity of your hands' — the intercessory dimension: one person's clean hands can secure another's deliverance. Eliphaz speaks truer than he knows: Job's prayer for his friends will effect their deliverance (42:8-9).
